facts_disclosed: Offered senior cultural liaison at Uluru, $15,000 more per year

turns.content: Do you know much about the differences between working for Parks in different states and territories. Like whether a transfer between parks works the same everywhere.

It depends on the jurisdictions involved. If you're moving within the same system -- say, both parks are managed by Parks Australia at the federal level -- a transfer is relatively straightforward. But if one park is state or territory managed and the other is federal, it's more like applying for a new position. Are you looking at a specific move?

I have been offered a job. Senior cultural liaison at Uluru. Different park, different part of the country. It came through Parks management so it is within the same system. But it would be a big move.
That's a significant opportunity. Senior cultural liaison at Uluru is a high-profile role. What's your initial feeling about it?
I go back and forth. Some mornings I think about it and I am ready to say yes. Other mornings I drive out on patrol and look at this country and I cannot imagine leaving it.
That kind of back and forth usually means there are genuine things pulling you in both directions. What are the main factors on each side for you?

The money is a big part. $15,000 more per year. I am raising my sister's two kids and that money would make a real difference. The older one, Tali, she needs better schooling and everything costs. And the title matters too. Senior cultural liaison. It recognises the work I have been doing for years without the formal title to go with it.

Those are both meaningful factors. $15,000 a year is substantial, especially with education costs ahead, and having your experience formally recognised in a senior title has professional value too. What's on the other side?

Uluru is Anangu country. I am Yolngu. That is not my wanga. If I go there I am a guest on someone else's country. I know how to do cultural liaison work but the cultural knowledge I carry is for this place. The songlines are different down there. The law is different. It is not just learning new tracks. It is a different way of knowing country entirely.

That's an important distinction. Being a cultural liaison relies on deep connection to the specific country and culture of a place. Having the professional skills but not the local cultural authority could put you in a complicated position. Do you know how the Anangu traditional owners feel about having someone from outside in that role?

I have not spoken to them. Parks made the offer. I think that is part of why they want someone like me. They want an Indigenous person with the formal qualifications and the ranger experience. There are not many of us who have both. But I would need to talk to the Anangu mob before I could even properly consider it. That is how it should work. The community has to be part of that decision.
That's a respectful approach. The community's acceptance is essential for a role like that to work properly. Have you talked to anyone else about it -- family, colleagues?

Not yet. My aunties would tell me to stay on my own country. That is what they would say without even thinking about it. But they are not the ones working out how to pay for things on a ranger salary. I have been sitting with it on my own. Thinking.
Sometimes sitting with a decision before involving others is the right thing to do, so you can figure out your own thoughts first. How would the move affect the kids?

That is the other weight on it. Bunjil is 10 and he has his friends here. He is just starting to find his feet after everything that happened. Tali is older, she might handle a move better, but she has her own things happening. Moving them to the desert, different school, different community. They have already been through enough without me pulling them across the country.

Stability matters a lot for kids who've already experienced upheaval. At the same time, you mentioned Tali needs better schooling options, and the extra income could help with that. It sounds like the decision affects the kids either way -- staying has costs and moving has costs.

That is exactly it. Both options cost something. If I stay, the money stays tight and Tali might miss out on what she needs for school. If I go, the kids lose their home and their connections here and I lose my country. There is no clean answer.
You're right that there's no clean answer. Some decisions don't have a right choice, just different trade-offs. Is there a deadline for giving Parks your answer?

End of the month. So I have a couple of weeks still. I keep turning it over. In the morning I think about the money and what it would mean for the kids. Then I go out on patrol and I look at this wanga and I think, this country has held me my whole life. How do I walk away from that. But then I think maybe that is selfish if the kids need something different.

Wanting to stay connected to your country isn't selfish. That connection is part of who you are and it shapes everything you do, including how you raise those kids. And wanting better opportunities for them isn't disloyal to the land either. These two things can both be true without one cancelling the other out.

I know. I just cannot see my way through it yet. Maybe I will not see it by thinking. Maybe it is the kind of thing that comes to you when you stop trying to work it out. I will take the kids out on country this weekend. Walk, be quiet, let the land talk.

That sounds like a good way to spend the weekend regardless of the decision. Sometimes stepping away from the problem is how the answer finds you. Whatever you decide, it's clear you're thinking about it carefully and for the right reasons.
Thank you. I have not said yes or no. I am still sitting with it. But it helped to say it out loud to someone.
Any time. I hope it becomes clearer for you. Take the time you have.
